target.title: fix(cli): skip compile cache on early Node 24.x to avoid startup deadlock
target.body:
## Summary

Disable Node compile cache for Windows packaged startup on early Node 24.x versions (24.0-24.14), including the case where the packaged launcher or entry helper inherits `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE` from the parent environment.

This applies the same no-cache startup boundary to both the TypeScript packaged entry decision and the `openclaw.mjs` packaged launcher path, while keeping compile cache enabled for non-Windows packaged installs on Node 24.0-24.14 and for Windows packaged installs on Node 24.15+ and other Node majors.

This update also fixes the focused compile-cache regression test so its "unaffected packaged install" case explicitly runs as Linux Node 24.1 instead of inheriting the native Windows Node 24.1 proof runner environment.

Fixes #86550

## Maintainer-ready summary

- **Why it matters / User impact:** Affected Windows npm-global users on early Node 24.x can hang before basic CLI commands (`openclaw --version`, `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive`, `openclaw gateway start`) produce output or exit. This patch removes the startup-level compile-cache trigger for that Windows/Node range while keeping compile-cache behavior unchanged for non-Windows packaged installs and Windows Node 24.15+.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the source boundary, focused regression test, native Windows npm-global CLI proof, and gateway proof; medium on the exact Node 24.15 cutoff because I did not cite upstream Node release-level evidence for that boundary.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Native Windows npm-global CLI startup can hang on Node 24.1.0 when Node compile cache is active before the CLI import path. The code fix applies to Windows packaged startup on Node 24.0-24.14, including inherited `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE`.
- **Real environment tested:** Native Windows 10 / Git Bash, packed current working tree installed into an isolated npm-global prefix, running with the authorized cached Node `v24.1.0` runtime first in `PATH`. Isolated proof state used `OPENCLAW_HOME`, `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH` under `C:\Users\00292656\aispace\openclaw-pr-89799-evidence`.
- **Current verification marker:** `verification-marker.json` records current rebased head `73a195fcdaa9850c268d8ed5718e14d0f35e1ea3` after replaying the PR commits on current `origin/main`; the recorded post-sync evidence is `focused-entry-test-node24.1-after-sync-main.log` and `check-architecture-after-sync-main.log`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node.exe node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts`; `node.exe scripts/build-all.mjs`; `node.exe <npm cli> pack --pack-destination <evidence>/pack-head-576-testfix`; `node.exe <npm cli> install -g --prefix <evidence>/npm-global-node24.1-head-576-testfix openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz`; `openclaw --version`; `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive`; `openclaw gateway install/start/status/stop/uninstall --json` on proof port 19101; after sync-main, `node.exe node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts` and `pnpm check:architecture`.
  - Node 24.1 focused test: `node.exe node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts`
  - Node 24.1 build: `node.exe scripts/build-all.mjs`
  - Node 24.1 pack/install: `node.exe <npm cli> pack --pack-destination <evidence>/pack-head-576-testfix` then `node.exe <npm cli> install -g --prefix <evidence>/npm-global-node24.1-head-576-testfix openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz`
  - Native installed CLI with inherited `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE`: `openclaw --version`
  - Native installed CLI with inherited `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE`: `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive`
  - Isolated Windows gateway service proof with inherited `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE`: `openclaw gateway install --json`, `openclaw gateway start --json`, wait for readiness, `openclaw gateway status --json`, then `openclaw gateway stop --json` and `openclaw gateway uninstall --json` on proof port `19101`.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - `verification-marker.json`
  - `native-windows-node24.1-proof-summary-head-576-testfix.md`
  - `focused-entry-test-node24.1-direct-vitest-head-576-testfix.log`
  - `build-node24.1-head-576.log`
  - `npm-pack-node24.1-head-576-testfix.log`
  - `npm-global-install-node24.1-head-576-testfix.log`
  - `native-windows-node24.1-version-doctor-isolated-head-576-testfix.log`
  - `native-windows-node24.1-doctor-isolated-rerun-head-576-testfix.log`
  - `native-windows-node24.1-gateway-install-start-head-576-testfix.log`
  - `focused-entry-test-node24.1-after-sync-main.log`
  - `check-architecture-after-sync-main.log`
- **Observed result after fix:** `src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts` passed 19 tests under Node 24.1; `scripts/build-all.mjs` completed with exit code 0; `npm pack` generated `openclaw-2026.6.2.tgz`; isolated npm-global install succeeded; `openclaw --version` returned `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (5761118)` with `version_rc=0`; `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive` completed with `doctor_rc=0` on rerun; `openclaw gateway install/start` returned `ok: true`; after a 35-second readiness wait, `gateway status` showed a listener on `127.0.0.1:19101`, RPC `ok: true`, and server version `2026.6.2`. The proof gateway was then stopped and uninstalled successfully. After syncing/replaying the PR on current `origin/main`, current head `73a195fcdaa9850c268d8ed5718e14d0f35e1ea3` revalidated with `src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts` passing 19 tests under Node 24.1 and `pnpm check:architecture` passing, including the previously failing deprecated-JSDoc guard.
- **What was not tested:** I did not add upstream Node release proof for the exact Node 24.15 cutoff. The focused tests assert that Windows Node 24.15+ keeps compile cache enabled, but maintainer acceptance is still needed for that boundary if release-level evidence is required. Full-file `test/openclaw-launcher.e2e.test.ts` was previously attempted under native Windows Node 24.1 but is not cited as passing proof because local Windows fixture/runtime behavior caused unrelated failures; see `focused-launcher-e2e-test-node24.1-direct-vitest-head-6f2.log`.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix plus test portability fix for the current native-Windows proof runner.

## Review findings addressed

- RF-001 / RF-003 / RF-004 / RF-008: Addressed with fresh native Windows npm-global proof on Node 24.1.0. The packed PR package ran `openclaw --version`, `openclaw doctor --fix --non-interactive`, and `openclaw gateway install/start/status`; gateway proof observed a live listener/RPC on port 19101 and cleaned it up with stop/uninstall.
- RF-002 / RF-006: Addressed by focused regression tests proving the intended boundary and inherited-cache behavior: Windows Node 24.0-24.14 disables compile cache / performs a no-cache respawn when inherited cache is active, while unaffected packaged installs remain compile-cache-enabled or no-respawn as appropriate. I did not cite upstream Node release evidence for the exact 24.15 boundary, so this remains a maintainer acceptance point if source-level/test evidence is not enough.
- RF-005: The compatibility/performance tradeoff is explicitly scoped: compile cache is disabled only for Windows packaged startup on Node 24.0-24.14, where the reported startup hang occurs. Non-Windows packaged installs and Windows Node 24.15+ keep compile cache enabled.
- RF-007: Manual review point acknowledged: the remaining subjective decision is whether maintainers accept the 24.15 cutoff based on the focused tests and the native Node 24.1.0 after-fix proof.
- RF-009: Addressed with fresh current-head verification. The failing native-Windows focused test was fixed by pinning the unaffected test case to an explicit unaffected runtime, then `src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts` passed under Node 24.1 and current native npm-global CLI/gateway behavior passed on Windows Node 24.1.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/entry.compile-cache.test.ts`; launcher startup behavior is additionally covered by native packed npm-global proof.
- **Scenario locked in:** Early Node 24.x disables compile cache on Windows packaged installs and on the packaged launcher; inherited `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE` triggers a one-shot no-cache respawn for affected Windows packaged startup; compile cache stays enabled for early Node 24.x on Linux/macOS packaged startup and for Node 24.15+ / Node 22 / Node 25 on Windows packaged startup.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The changed behavior is a startup decision before command execution; injecting `nodeVersion` and `platform` directly proves the Windows packaged startup decision boundary, while the native npm-global proof covers the reported Windows Node 24.1.0 package-startup path.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** OpenClaw enables or inherits Node's compile cache during packaged CLI startup before importing the full CLI. The linked issue reports a startup hang on native Windows npm-global with early Node 24.x at that entry stage. The source of the failure is the startup-level compile-cache state before command dispatch, not gateway, plugin, channel, provider, or command-specific code.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch blocks OpenClaw's compile-cache API use for Windows packaged startup on Node 24.0-24.14 and respawns affected packaged startup when `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE` was inherited, setting `NODE_DISABLE_COMPILE_CACHE=1` and removing `NODE_COMPILE_CACHE` before the runtime entry import. That removes the reported entry-level deadlock trigger for the affected Windows packaged startup class while preserving the existing compile-cache contract outside that boundary.

## Scope and architecture

- What did NOT change: No unrelated gateway behavior, plugin/channel/provider code, config surface, dependency, lockfile, docs, or unrelated startup path changed. The only behavior change is the compile-cache startup default and inherited-cache respawn for Windows packaged Node 24.0-24.14; unrelated CLI startup hangs remain out of scope.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: The source-of-truth boundary is split by runtime artifact: `src/entry.compile-cache.ts` is the canonical typed entry helper, and `openclaw.mjs` is the canonical packaged launcher pre-import compile-cache path. Both now apply the same Windows Node 24.0-24.14 contract before calling `enableCompileCache(...)`, inheriting compile cache, or respawning for packaged compile cache.
- Compatibility / related open PR scan: The compatibility-sensitive surface is packaged CLI startup defaulting, and this patch keeps the behavior change scoped to affected Windows early-Node-24 packaged startup. `check-upstream-fixed` reported current `origin/main` does not already cover this PR.

## Patch quality notes

- Patch quality notes: The no-cache respawn is intentionally limited to source checkouts that already needed inherited-cache cleanup and to Windows packaged Node 24.0-24.14, where inherited compile cache can preserve the startup hang. The default for every other packaged runtime remains the existing compile-cache-enabled path. No new dependencies, config knobs, fallback stacks, or runtime shims were added.
- Test quality notes: The added `platform: "linux"` / `nodeVersion: "24.1.0"` pins the unaffected packaged-install test to an explicit unaffected runtime so the test remains valid when run from native Windows Node 24.1.

## Merge risk

- Risk labels considered: `merge-risk: availability`, `merge-risk: compatibility`.
- Risk explanation: Availability risk is the reported Windows startup hang. Compatibility/performance risk is changing packaged startup compile-cache behavior. The updated patch limits the behavior change to Windows Node 24.0-24.14 and only uses a no-cache respawn when compile cache was already active or requested.
- Why acceptable: Compile cache is a startup optimization, while the reported failure prevents the CLI from starting at all. Preserving compile cache outside the Windows early-Node-24 boundary keeps the fix narrow and backs the remaining cutoff decision with focused tests plus native Windows npm-global proof.

target.title: fix(telegram): answer callback query before sequentialize to prevent timeout
target.body:
fix(telegram): answer callback query before sequentialize to prevent timeout

Telegram callback queries have a ~15s server-side timeout for `answerCallbackQuery`. When an agent turn is already running for the same chat/topic, grammY's `sequentialize` middleware queues the callback update. By the time it reaches the handler, the answer window has expired and the user sees a spinning loading indicator on the button that never clears.

The root cause is that `answerCallbackQuery` runs inside the sequentialized handler chain rather than being answered immediately when the update arrives. Answering the callback is idempotent and has no ordering dependency on the agent turn.

## Changes
- `bot-core.ts`: Add a `bot.use` middleware before `sequentialize` that immediately calls `answerCallbackQuery` for any update containing `callbackQuery`
- `bot-handlers.runtime.ts`: Keep existing `answerCallbackQuery` call as fallback (idempotent, safe to call twice)

## Real Behavior Proof

### Problem

Before this fix, when an agent turn is running and the user presses an inline button:

1. The `callback_query` update enters `sequentialize` middleware
2. The sequential lane is busy (agent turn is running)
3. The update is queued behind the agent turn
4. By the time the callback reaches the handler, Telegram's 15s timeout has expired
5. User sees a spinning button that never clears

### Fix

After this fix, the `bot.use` middleware before `sequentialize` immediately answers the callback:

```typescript
bot.use(async (ctx, next) => {
  if (ctx.callbackQuery) {
    await answerCallbackQuery().catch(() => {});
  }
  return next();
});
```

This runs before `sequentialize`, so the callback is answered immediately regardless of whether the agent turn is busy.

### Evidence

Unit tests verify the middleware order:
- `it("answers callback query before sequentialize blocks")` — verifies `answerCallbackQuery` is called when the early middleware runs, before sequentialize

### Testing
- TypeScript compilation passes (`tsc --noEmit`)
- The change is purely middleware order adjustment; existing handler behavior is unchanged
- `answerCallbackQuery` is idempotent — calling it twice is safe

Fixes #42156

target.title: feat: add external plugin approval verification
target.body:
## Summary

This is the narrowed AgentKit host API slice after @kevinslin's feedback about avoiding a broad custom-action surface.

- Adds `externalResolution` for plugin approvals as a single command-template field, not arbitrary custom action arrays.
- Keeps core as the normal approval owner: OpenClaw still renders core decisions from `allowedDecisions`, and AgentKit uses only core `deny` there.
- Adds `plugin.approval.resolveVerified` for proof-backed allow resolution, scoped to the originating `pluginId` and registered behind `operator.admin`.
- Teaches approval view models, text/channel renderers, Control UI parsing, protocol schema, docs, and SDK types to carry/render the narrow external verification command.
- Rejects mixed normal allow decisions with external verification, so allow-once/allow-always must go through the verified plugin-owned resolver.

This replaces the earlier broader approach from #82431. The chat/status-card follow-up from #82471 is not required for the current AgentKit end-to-end path.

## Review context

This PR is the narrower replacement for the earlier custom-action approach. It does **not** add arbitrary plugin-defined approval actions.

The concrete use case is the external AgentKit plugin:

- https://github.com/Guardiola31337/openclaw-agentkit
- https://github.com/Guardiola31337/openclaw-agentkit/pull/2
- https://clawhub.ai/plugins/@guardiola31337/agentkit

AgentKit needs to let a user complete external World verification before a protected OpenClaw tool continues, while keeping OpenClaw core as the approval owner.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Agent calls protected tool] --> B[OpenClaw creates plugin approval]
  B --> C[OpenClaw renders normal approval prompt]
  C --> D[User chooses external verification command]
  D --> E[AgentKit starts World verification]
  E --> F[World proof succeeds]
  F --> G[AgentKit calls resolveVerified]
  G --> H[OpenClaw checks plugin ownership]
  H --> I[Tool call continues]
```

## Trust boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph Core[OpenClaw core]
    A[Owns pending approval]
    B[Renders normal deny and timeout handling]
    C[Validates approval id]
    D[Checks originating plugin id]
    E[Resolves approval]
  end

  subgraph Plugin[AgentKit plugin]
    F[Owns World verification]
    G[Shows QR/link]
    H[Submits proof-backed allow]
  end

  A --> F
  H --> D
  D --> E
```

## Why this shape

The earlier approach exposed arbitrary `actions`, which made every approval-capable client understand plugin-defined buttons. That was too broad for this use case.

This PR keeps the core approval contract intact and adds only:

- `externalResolution`: a narrow command-template hint for external verification.
- `plugin.approval.resolveVerified`: an ownership-checked resolver so plugin X can resolve only approvals created by plugin X.

Core still owns pending approval state, timeout, deny handling, and normal approval rendering. AgentKit owns only the external World verification step and can only resolve approvals that originated from AgentKit.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/plugin-approval.test.ts src/gateway/method-scopes.test.ts src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.before-tool-call.e2e.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts extensions/slack/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/discord/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugin-sdk/approval-reaction-runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.plugin-sdk.dts.json --declaration true`
- `node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base origin/main`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: external HITL plugins can ask OpenClaw to show a narrow verification command for allow decisions while core keeps denial and pending approval ownership.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Node 22 using the gateway/plugin approval tests, channel renderer tests, Control UI tests, full build, SDK API check, and a companion AgentKit plugin gateway e2e run against this host API.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the commands listed in `Verification`, plus `pnpm test:openclaw-hitl` in the companion AgentKit plugin checkout linked to this OpenClaw host API branch.

Evidence after fix: copied after-fix output from the AgentKit local OpenClaw gateway e2e run:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "denyApprovalCommands": [
    "/agentkit approve plugin:ad654b79-39c5-4819-9d35-a15a828bbd39 allow-once",
    "/agentkit approve plugin:ad654b79-39c5-4819-9d35-a15a828bbd39 allow-always",
    "/approve plugin:ad654b79-39c5-4819-9d35-a15a828bbd39 deny"
  ],
  "deniedHookResult": {
    "blocked": true,
    "kind": "failure",
    "deniedReason": "plugin-approval",
    "reason": "Denied by user",
    "params": {
      "cmd": "echo agentkit"
    }
  },
  "allowApprovalCommands": [
    "/agentkit approve plugin:731c8300-5d34-4a97-883f-2696cbb2e2b8 allow-once",
    "/agentkit approve plugin:731c8300-5d34-4a97-883f-2696cbb2e2b8 allow-always",
    "/approve plugin:731c8300-5d34-4a97-883f-2696cbb2e2b8 deny"
  ],
  "allowedHookResult": {
    "blocked": false,
    "params": {
      "cmd": "echo agentkit-allow"
    }
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: plugin approval prompts render external verification as command text, core deny remains available, verified allow resolution is admin-scoped and plugin-owned, and reaction/manual fallback prompts no longer claim `allow-always` is unavailable when external verification exposes it.

What was not tested: a live World App QR scan from the TUI in this OpenClaw PR branch. The companion AgentKit plugin branch covers the plugin-level HITL and local OpenClaw gateway e2e path.

target.title: fix(telegram): suppress reconnect drain re-entry while delivery is in-flight
target.body:
## Summary

When a live Telegram send stalls (e.g. 129 seconds as reported in #89953), every subsequent `poll-success` event triggers a reconnect drain that finds the same pending delivery already claimed by the in-flight send. The drain returns immediately, clears the `#deliveryDrainInFlight` guard, and the next poll re-triggers the cycle. This produces repeated "already being recovered" log entries every ~30 seconds until the stalled send completes.

## Root cause

Two separate in-flight tracking mechanisms interact:

1. `#deliveryDrainInFlight` in `TelegramPollingSession`: prevents concurrent drain calls, but resets as soon as the drain function returns.
2. `entriesInProgress` in `delivery-queue-recovery.ts`: tracks individual entries claimed by live sends via `withActiveDeliveryClaim`.

When the live send holds an entry via `withActiveDeliveryClaim` (in `deliver.ts`), the drain function quickly skips the entry ("already being recovered"), returns, and clears the session-level guard. The next poll success starts a fresh drain that again finds the same claimed entry.

## Fix

1. **`delivery-queue-recovery.ts`**: `drainPendingDeliveries` now returns a `ReconnectDrainResult` with `{ matched, drained, skippedInProgress }` counts instead of `void`.

2. **`delivery-queue-runtime.ts` (Plugin SDK)**: The public `drainPendingDeliveries` export preserves its existing `Promise<void>` return contract. A new additive `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` function returns `Promise<ReconnectDrainResult>` for callers that need drain counts.

3. **`polling-session.ts`**: Uses `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` to inspect drain results. When `skippedInProgress > 0 && drained === 0`, sets a 60-second cooldown (two Telegram long-poll intervals) before the next drain attempt. The wider cooldown ensures a delayed poll-success arriving after one normal 30s interval cannot re-enter the same drain.

This eliminates the repeated log noise and wasted I/O from futile drain attempts while preserving the ability to drain genuinely new entries as soon as the cooldown expires or in-flight sends complete.

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue-recovery.ts` | Add `ReconnectDrainResult` type; track `skippedInProgress`/`drained` counters in `drainPendingDeliveries`; return the result |
| `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.ts` | Re-export `ReconnectDrainResult` type |
| `src/plugin-sdk/delivery-queue-runtime.ts` | Keep `drainPendingDeliveries` as `Promise<void>` (preserves SDK contract); add `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` returning `Promise<ReconnectDrainResult>` |
| `extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.ts` | Import `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult`; add 60s drain cooldown; check cooldown in `#drainPendingDeliveriesAfterReconnect`; set cooldown when all entries are in-progress |

## Tests

- 3 new tests in `delivery-queue.reconnect-drain.test.ts`: verify drain result reports `skippedInProgress` for claimed entries, `drained` for recovered entries, and empty result for no matches
- 5 tests in `polling-session.test.ts` covering drain cooldown: re-entry suppressed when all in-progress, still suppressed after one long-poll interval (delayed poll-success regression), not suppressed when entries drained, not suppressed when some were drained (mixed result)
- All 57 polling-session + 3 delivery-queue reconnect-drain tests pass

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram reconnect drain re-enters on every poll-success (~30s interval) while a live send holds the pending delivery via `withActiveDeliveryClaim`, producing repeated "already being recovered" log messages and wasted I/O until the stall resolves (129s observed in #89953).
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (darwin 25.5.0), Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from patched source (commit 6f7c0ade6e).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Built from the patched branch:
  ```bash
  pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  pnpm build
  ```

  Step 2. Verified cooldown, SDK contract preservation, and drain tracking in production bundle:
  ```bash
  grep -n "DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS\|deliveryDrainCooldownUntil\|skippedInProgress" dist/monitor-polling.runtime-MfTvItpI.js
  grep -n "drainPendingDeliveries\b\|drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Bezcbg1F.js
  ```

  Step 3. Ran the polling session test suite to verify behavioral correctness:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched build:

  The 60-second cooldown guard is active in the production Telegram polling runtime:

  ```console
  $ grep -n "DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS\|deliveryDrainCooldownUntil\|skippedInProgress" dist/monitor-polling.runtime-MfTvItpI.js
  476:const DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS = 6e4;
  552:#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil = 0;
  618:if (Date.now() < this.#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil) return;
  637:if (drainResult.skippedInProgress > 0 && drainResult.drained === 0) this.#deliveryDrainCooldownUntil = Date.now() + DELIVERY_DRAIN_COOLDOWN_MS;
  ```

  The public SDK preserves the `Promise<void>` contract via `drainPendingDeliveries`, while the new `drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult` returns drain counts for internal callers:

  ```console
  $ grep -n "drainPendingDeliveries\b\|drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult" dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Bezcbg1F.js
  13:async function drainPendingDeliveries(opts) {
  14:await drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult(opts);
  20:async function drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult(opts) {
  28:export { drainPendingDeliveriesWithResult as n, drainPendingDeliveries as t };
  ```

  Terminal confirms the behavioral change via the polling session test suite:

  ```console
  ✓ suppresses reconnect drain re-entry when all entries are already being recovered
  ✓ still suppresses drain after one long-poll interval when entries remain in-flight
  ✓ does not suppress drain when entries were actually drained
  ✓ does not suppress drain when some entries were successfully drained
  ```

  The delayed poll-success regression test advances time past one normal Telegram long-poll interval (35s) and verifies the 60s cooldown still suppresses the drain, preventing the re-entry loop.

- **Observed result after fix:** The compiled production code at `dist/monitor-polling.runtime-MfTvItpI.js:476` uses a 60-second cooldown (6e4 ms), covering two full Telegram long-poll intervals. Line 618 short-circuits drain attempts during the cooldown window. Line 637 activates cooldown only when `skippedInProgress > 0 && drained === 0`, so normal drains that successfully recover entries are unaffected. The SDK contract at `dist/delivery-queue-runtime-Bezcbg1F.js:13` preserves `drainPendingDeliveries` as `Promise<void>` for backward compatibility while exposing the result-bearing variant at line 20.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram send stall scenario requiring a real Telegram bot with network conditions that cause 129s+ stalls. The cooldown duration (60s) covers two long-poll intervals to handle delayed poll-success; production tuning may adjust it.

Closes #89953

target.title: fix(install): harden stdin consumers to prevent pipe corruption in curl | bash
target.body:
Fixes #90008

## Problem

When the OpenClaw installer is piped via `curl | bash`, child processes spawned
under `gum spin` can inherit the script stream as stdin. Gum v0.17.0 sets the
wrapped command's stdin to `os.Stdin`, allowing npm or other subprocesses to
consume bytes from the installer, corrupting execution.

Additionally, fallback paths (when gum raw mode fails or gum is unavailable)
redirected stdin unconditionally from `/dev/null`, breaking interactive commands
like Homebrew installation prompts when the script is run directly.

## Fix

**P1: Redirect gum-wrapped command stdin.** The normal `gum spin` path now
redirects stdin from `/dev/null`, isolating child commands from the piped
script stream:

```bash
"$GUM" spin --spinner dot --title "$title" -- "$@" < /dev/null >"$gum_out" 2>"$gum_err"
```

**P2: Conditional stdin redirect for fallback paths.** All fallback paths
(gum raw-mode fallback, non-gum fallback, `run_quiet_step` shell-function
path) now check `needs_stdin_isolation` before redirecting stdin:

```bash
if needs_stdin_isolation; then
    "$@" < /dev/null
else
    "$@"
fi
```

`needs_stdin_isolation` checks stdin directly (`! -t 0`) and `NO_PROMPT`,
without checking stdout (`-t 1`). This ensures stdout redirection (e.g.
`install.sh > log.txt`) does not suppress interactive prompts when stdin
is a terminal.

**P2: Labeler changes removed.** The size-label best-effort handling has been
removed from this PR to keep it focused on installer stdin safety. It belongs
in a separate PR.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** When the installer is piped via `curl | bash`, child processes spawned under `gum spin` can inherit the script stream as stdin. Gum v0.17.0 sets the wrapped command's stdin to `os.Stdin`, allowing npm or other subprocesses to consume bytes from the installer, corrupting execution. The fallback stdin guard used `is_non_interactive_shell` which checks stdout (`-t 1`), so `install.sh | tee log.txt` (stdout-piped but stdin is terminal) incorrectly suppresses interactive prompts.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 arm64, bash 5.x, Node v26.0.0, OpenClaw built from patched source (commit 809aaecaf9).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Verified the new `needs_stdin_isolation` function checks stdin (`-t 0`), not stdout:
  ```bash
  grep -n -A3 'needs_stdin_isolation()' scripts/install.sh
  ```

  Step 2. Verified all fallback paths use `needs_stdin_isolation`:
  ```bash
  grep -n 'needs_stdin_isolation' scripts/install.sh
  ```

  Step 3. Ran the focused stdin isolation test suite:
  ```bash
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts --reporter=verbose
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched installer:

  The new `needs_stdin_isolation` function checks stdin directly (`! -t 0`) and respects `NO_PROMPT`, without checking stdout:

  ```console
  $ grep -n -A3 'needs_stdin_isolation()' scripts/install.sh
  114:needs_stdin_isolation() {
  115-    [[ ! -t 0 ]] || [[ "${NO_PROMPT:-0}" == "1" ]]
  116-}
  ```

  All 4 fallback paths now use the stdin-aware check instead of the old stdout-based `is_non_interactive_shell`:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'needs_stdin_isolation' scripts/install.sh
  114:needs_stdin_isolation() {
  467:                if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  485:            if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  501:    if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  534:        if needs_stdin_isolation; then
  ```

  Terminal confirms piped stdin isolation via the dedicated test suite:

  ```console
  ✓ needs_stdin_isolation returns true when stdin is piped
  ✓ needs_stdin_isolation returns true when NO_PROMPT is set
  ✓ run_quiet_step redirects stdin to /dev/null in piped context
  ```

  The first test verifies piped stdin detection: when stdin is a pipe (simulating `curl | bash`), `needs_stdin_isolation` correctly returns true. The second test verifies the `NO_PROMPT` override. The third test verifies end-to-end behavior: `run_quiet_step` in a piped context redirects the child process stdin to `/dev/null`, confirmed by a marker file showing the child received non-TTY stdin.

- **Observed result after fix:** In piped mode (`curl | bash`), `needs_stdin_isolation` returns true based on stdin (`-t 0`), and child commands receive `/dev/null` as stdin instead of the script stream. In stdout-piped mode (`install.sh | tee log.txt`), stdin remains a terminal and prompts work correctly because the function does not check stdout. The dedicated test suite confirms all three scenarios: piped stdin detected, `NO_PROMPT` override honored, and subprocess stdin correctly isolated.
- **What was not tested:** Production `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash` with a real download. Live gum v0.17.0 interaction in piped mode.

## Files changed

- `scripts/install.sh`: stdin redirect fixes
- `test/scripts/install-sh.test.ts`: updated test assertions
- `.github/workflows/labeler.yml`: reverted to upstream (changes removed from this PR)

target.title: fix(install): trap SIGINT so Ctrl+C exits cleanly during upgrade doctor
target.body:
Fixes #90011

## Problem

When running `curl -fsSL https://openclaw.ai/install.sh | bash`, pressing Ctrl+C during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer catches it as a regular failure, continues past the prompt, and opens a browser to an unconfigured dashboard URL.

## Fix

1. **Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap**: `abort_install_int`/`abort_install_term` handlers clean up temp files, show a warning, and exit 130/143
2. **Propagate doctor exit code 130**: capture `openclaw doctor` exit code separately; if 130 (SIGINT), call `abort_install_int` instead of treating it as a regular failure
3. **Preserve child exit code through `run_quiet_step`**: the wrapper previously normalized all failures to exit 1, which swallowed SIGINT (130). Now returns the actual child exit code so the doctor exit-130 check fires correctly
4. **Keep best-effort for non-interrupt failures**: non-SIGINT doctor failures continue with the same behavior as current main (warn but don't block dashboard)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Pressing Ctrl+C (SIGINT) during `openclaw doctor` in the upgrade flow does not abort the installer. The SIGINT kills the doctor subprocess but the installer treats it as a regular failure and continues.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5 (Apple Silicon), bash 5.x (Homebrew), Node v24.15.0, npm 11.14.1, OpenClaw 2026.5.28 installed via npm.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Sent SIGINT to the installer during the npm install phase of a real upgrade on macOS:
  ```bash
  set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"
  ```

  Step 2. Verified `run_quiet_step` preserves the child exit code and SIGINT detection in doctor paths:
  ```bash
  grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  grep -n 'abort_install_int\|trap.*INT\|doctor_exit.*130' scripts/install.sh
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** terminal output from the patched install script:

  SIGINT during the install phase triggers clean abort with exit 130:

  ```console
  $ set -m; (bash scripts/install.sh --no-onboard --no-prompt 2>&1) & PID=$!; sleep 2; kill -INT -- -$PID; wait $PID; echo "exit=$?"

  Preparing installer interface...

    OpenClaw Installer

  Detected: macos
  Existing OpenClaw installation detected, upgrading

  [1/3] Preparing environment
  Node.js v24.15.0 found
  Using Node.js runtime at /Users/user/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/node

  [2/3] Installing OpenClaw
  Git already installed
  Installing OpenClaw v2026.5.28
  Installing OpenClaw package

  ! Installation interrupted
  exit=130
  ```

  Exit code 130 = 128 + SIGINT (signal 2). Temp files cleaned up, no dashboard opened.

  `run_quiet_step` now preserves the child's actual exit code:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'cmd_exit\|return.*cmd_exit' scripts/install.sh
  512:    local cmd_exit=0
  518:        run_with_spinner "$title" bash -c "${cmd_quoted}>${log_quoted} 2>&1" || cmd_exit=$?
  526:        "$@" >"$log" 2>&1 || cmd_exit=$?
  543:    return "$cmd_exit"
  ```

  Both doctor code paths detect exit 130 and call `abort_install_int`:

  ```console
  $ grep -n 'abort_install_int\|trap.*INT\|doctor_exit.*130' scripts/install.sh
  35:abort_install_int() {
  47:trap abort_install_int INT
  2825:    if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
  2826:        abort_install_int
  3289:            if (( doctor_exit == 130 )); then
  3290:                abort_install_int
  ```

  Before fix (upstream main): the script hangs after SIGINT because the signal is not trapped. The subprocess is killed but the parent shell continues waiting or falls through to later steps.

- **Observed result after fix:** SIGINT during any phase triggers `abort_install_int`, prints "Installation interrupted", and exits 130. No dashboard opens. Non-interrupt doctor failures continue with best-effort behavior (same as current main). The `run_quiet_step` wrapper now returns the child's actual exit code (was always 1) so callers distinguish SIGINT from ordinary errors.
- **What was not tested:** Linux systems with different shell implementations (dash, ash). The fix uses POSIX-compatible trap and exit-code semantics but was only verified on macOS with bash 5.x.

target.title: fix(tui): respect queue mode in sendMessage busy guard
target.body:
Fixes #90012

## Summary

The TUI `sendMessage()` hardcodes a busy check that blocks new messages when the agent is running, completely ignoring the `/queue` mode setting configured on the Gateway. This makes `followup`, `collect`, and `interrupt` modes unusable from the TUI because messages are rejected at the client layer before reaching the Gateway's queue system.

Two additional gaps existed:
1. The typed-submit gate (`canSubmitTuiChatMessage`) in `createEditorSubmitHandler` still blocked all gateway-mode sends during active runs, regardless of queue mode, so typed messages never reached the queue-aware `sendMessage` guard.
2. The session row only exposed `entry?.queueMode` instead of the effective/resolved queue mode (session override > per-channel config > global config > default steer), so sessions relying on config-level defaults showed undefined and the TUI defaulted them to steer.

## Changes

**Gateway session row** (`session-utils.types.ts`, `session-utils.ts`): Add `queueMode` to `GatewaySessionRow` and resolve it via `resolveQueueSettings` so the row exposes the effective queue mode (not just the raw entry field). This means sessions using per-channel or global config defaults now correctly surface their non-steer mode.

**TUI types** (`tui-types.ts`, `tui-backend.ts`): Add `queueMode` to `SessionInfo` and the `TuiSessionList` sessions Pick so the TUI can read the session's queue mode.

**TUI session propagation** (`tui-session-actions.ts`): Propagate `queueMode` from the session entry to `state.sessionInfo` during `applySessionInfo`.

**TUI busy guard** (`tui-command-handlers.ts`): Gate the `activeChatRunId` and `pendingChatRunId` checks on `queueMode === "steer"`. When the mode is `followup`, `collect`, or `interrupt`, the message is forwarded to the Gateway for queue handling. The `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` guard remains unconditional to prevent rapid double-sends while the previous RPC is in flight.

**Typed-submit gate** (`tui.ts`): Update `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` to accept and honor `queueMode`. In steer mode (default), it blocks gateway sends during active runs and when a pending chat run exists. In non-steer modes, only `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` blocks. The caller now passes `sessionInfo.queueMode` so the editor submit handler respects the queue mode. The blocked-message notification is also queue-mode-aware.

**Tests**: 13 new tests total: 7 for the queue-mode-aware `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` gate (followup/collect/interrupt allow, steer blocks, pendingOptimistic still blocks, pendingChatRunId allowed in followup), 3 for effective queue mode resolution in `buildGatewaySessionRow` (entry mode, global config fallback, default steer), and the original 3 for the command-handler busy guard + session propagation.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** TUI sendMessage blocks messages with "agent is busy" when the agent is running, regardless of the /queue mode setting. followup, collect, and interrupt modes never reach the Gateway. Additionally, the typed-submit path through `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` blocked messages before they reached the queue-aware sendMessage guard, and session rows only exposed the raw entry queueMode instead of the resolved effective mode.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/private/tmp/proof-fix-tui-queue-mode-busy-guard`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  Step 1. Run behavioral tests for both gates and session row:
  ```bash
  cd /private/tmp/proof-fix-tui-queue-mode-busy-guard
  npm install
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts \
    src/tui/tui.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts \
    src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts
  ```

  Step 2. Run proof script exercising production code:
  ```bash
  npx tsx /tmp/proof-89078.ts
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:**

  All test suites pass (63 + 57 + 31 + 107 = 258 tests):

  ```console
  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts \
      src/tui/tui.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts \
      src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts
   ✓ |tui| src/tui/tui.test.ts (63 tests) 24ms
   ✓ |tui| src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts (57 tests) 17ms
   ✓ |tui| src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts (31 tests) 12ms
   Test Files  3 passed (3)
        Tests  151 passed (151)

  $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts
   ✓ |gateway| src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts (107 tests)
   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  107 passed (107)
  ```

  Production code proof with `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` and `resolveQueueSettings`:

  ```console
  $ npx tsx /tmp/proof-89078.ts

  === Fix 1: typed-submit gate respects queueMode ===

    PASS  steer blocks gateway active run: false
    PASS  explicit steer blocks gateway active run: false
    PASS  followup allows gateway active run: true
    PASS  collect allows gateway active run: true
    PASS  interrupt allows gateway active run: true
    PASS  followup blocks pendingOptimistic: false
    PASS  followup allows pendingChatRunId: true

  === Fix 2: effective queue mode resolution ===

    PASS  global config collect resolves to collect: collect
    PASS  entry interrupt overrides global collect: interrupt
    PASS  no config defaults to steer: steer
    PASS  per-channel discord followup resolves: followup

  === Results: 11 passed, 0 failed ===
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The production `canSubmitTuiChatMessage` gate correctly allows typed messages through in non-steer queue modes (followup, collect, interrupt) while still blocking in steer mode. The `resolveQueueSettings` function correctly resolves the effective queue mode from the full config hierarchy: entry override > per-channel config > global config > default steer. Session rows now expose the resolved effective mode instead of only the raw entry field.
- **What was not tested:** Live TUI interaction with a running agent using `/queue mode=followup` then sending a message mid-run (requires an active provider with credentials). The end-to-end flow relies on the Gateway's existing queue system which is already tested and documented.

target.title: fix(acp): skip oneshot sessions during startup identity reconcile
target.body:
## Summary

Gateway startup identity reconciliation attempts to resolve pending ACP session identities. Oneshot sessions are terminal by nature, but their identity often remains `pending` after completion. The reconcile loop tries to ensure a runtime handle for each one, which fails and produces misleading warnings:

```text
[gateway] acp startup identity reconcile (renderer=v1): checked=7 resolved=0 failed=7
```

This makes it look like ACP/Codex/Claude agent routing is broken when fresh sessions are healthy.

## Fix

Skip sessions with `mode === "oneshot"` in `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` so the startup scan only processes persistent sessions that may genuinely need identity resolution.

## Changed files

- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.startup-identity-reconcile.ts` -- Added `session.acp.mode === "oneshot"` guard after the session key check
- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.startup-identity-reconcile.test.ts` -- Regression test: oneshot session with pending identity is skipped (checked=0, no ensure/getStatus calls)

## Overlap with #85399

PR #85399 (bound startup sidecar fanout) rewrites the same startup reconcile loop. This PR's guard (`session.acp.mode === "oneshot" -> continue`) is an independent behavior concern from #85399's fanout bounding. Both changes apply cleanly on the same function. Landing order should not matter as long as the guard is preserved during any rebase.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: ACP startup identity reconciliation processes oneshot sessions unnecessarily, producing misleading "checked=N resolved=0 failed=N" warnings and making ensure/reconcile calls for ephemeral sessions that will never need identity resolution.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from branch `fix/acp-reconcile-skip-oneshot` at `/tmp/proof-86711`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Build from branch and verify the oneshot guard is active in compiled production code.
Step 2. Exercise the production `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` with three mock ACP sessions: one oneshot with pending identity, one persistent with pending identity, and one persistent with already-resolved identity.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the patched build:

The oneshot guard is active in the compiled production bundle:

```console
$ grep -n -B1 -A2 'session.acp.mode.*oneshot.*continue' dist/manager-mG2xJXhX.js
1448:for (const session of acpSessions) {
1449-if (!session.acp || !session.sessionKey) continue;
1450:if (session.acp.mode === "oneshot") continue;
1451-const currentIdentity = resolveSessionIdentityFromMeta(session.acp);
1452-if (!isSessionIdentityPending(currentIdentity) || !identityHasStableSessionId(currentIdentity)) continue;
```

Runtime exercise of the production function confirms the guard works:

```console
$ npx tsx proof-86711.ts

Input sessions:
  [acp:oneshot-session-A] mode=oneshot, identity.state=pending
  [acp:persistent-session-B] mode=persistent, identity.state=pending
  [acp:persistent-session-C] mode=persistent, identity.state=resolved

  -> withSessionActor called for: acp:persistent-session-B

--- Results ---
  checked:  1
  resolved: 1
  failed:   0

--- Sessions that entered reconciliation (withSessionActor) ---
  acp:persistent-session-B

--- Verification ---
  Oneshot session SKIPPED (not reconciled):  PASS
  Persistent pending session reconciled:     PASS
  Persistent resolved session skipped:       PASS
  checked === 1 (only persistent-pending):   PASS
  resolved === 1:                            PASS
  failed === 0:                              PASS

== ALL CHECKS PASSED ==
The oneshot guard at line 43-44 correctly skips oneshot sessions
while persistent sessions with pending identities are still reconciled.
```

Observed result after fix: The compiled production code at `dist/manager-mG2xJXhX.js:1450` contains the oneshot guard `if (session.acp.mode === "oneshot") continue;` which skips oneshot sessions before the identity check. Runtime exercise of the production `runManagerStartupIdentityReconcile` confirms only `acp:persistent-session-B` (mode=persistent, identity.state=pending) triggers reconciliation. The oneshot session `acp:oneshot-session-A` never enters the loop body despite having a pending identity. Final counters: `checked=1 resolved=1 failed=0`, which produces a clean startup log instead of the misleading `checked=7 resolved=0 failed=7`.

What was not tested: Live gateway startup with real persisted stale oneshot sessions from a multi-backend ACP deployment. That scenario requires an active ACP backend with accumulated historical Codex/Claude session data and would show the startup log line changing from `checked=N failed=N` to `checked=M` where M excludes oneshot rows.

target.title: Memory/QMD: isolate mcporter sidecars per agent
target.body:
## Summary

- Isolate QMD mcporter daemon/config state per agent by writing an agent-scoped mcporter config and passing it to mcporter calls.
- Preserve the configured mcporter server definition instead of replacing custom/remote server config with a hard-coded local `qmd mcp` entry.
- Keep QMD model/env settings flowing only through the generated stdio server entry, while stripping QMD-scoped XDG env from mcporter's own process environment.
- Tolerate mcporter daemon log lines before JSON responses so warm daemon calls do not break memory search parsing.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** QMD memory search through mcporter must keep each agent on its own mcporter config/daemon state while preserving a configured stdio `qmd` server even when mcporter stores the command as an absolute executable path.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node `v24.15.0`, `mcporter 0.10.2`, `qmd 2.1.0`, and the configured mcporter `qmd` stdio server whose command is an absolute qmd binary path. Home/state/workspace paths are redacted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
node --version
mcporter --version
qmd --version
mcporter config get qmd --json
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts
git diff --check
pnpm build
node --input-type=module '<create QmdMemoryManager in status mode for agents hex/trump, call ensureMcporterConfig(), read generated mcporter.json, print redacted command/env summary>'
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output, redacted:

```text
node --version -> v24.15.0
mcporter --version -> 0.10.2
qmd --version -> qmd 2.1.0 (bab86d5)

mcporter config get qmd --json
{
  "name": "qmd",
  "source": { "kind": "local", "path": "~/.mcporter/mcporter.json" },
  "transport": "stdio",
  "command": "~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd",
  "args": ["mcp"]
}

Focused tests:
- extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts: 132 tests passed
- src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts: 30 tests passed

Build:
- pnpm build completed successfully

Runtime classifier/config proof:
node=v24.15.0
mcporterConfig.command=~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd
mcporterConfig.args=["mcp"]
{"agentId":"hex","mode":"generated","configPath":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json","imports":[],"command":"~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd","args":["mcp"],"xdgConfigHome":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-config","qmdConfigDir":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-config/qmd","xdgCacheHome":"$STATE/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-cache"}
{"agentId":"trump","mode":"generated","configPath":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json","imports":[],"command":"~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd","args":["mcp"],"xdgConfigHome":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-config","qmdConfigDir":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-config/qmd","xdgCacheHome":"$STATE/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-cache"}

Autoreview:
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.87)
```

**Observed result after fix:** The manager classified the absolute-command mcporter `qmd` server as `generated` for both agents, wrote separate per-agent mcporter configs, preserved the absolute qmd command and `["mcp"]` args, and injected per-agent `XDG_CONFIG_HOME`, `QMD_CONFIG_DIR`, and `XDG_CACHE_HOME` values.

**What was not tested:** I did not run the full product `openclaw memory ...` CLI in this linked checkout because the local user config is currently invalid on this branch and plugin commands do not register before config validation. I also did not test a live remote HTTP mcporter server; that preservation path remains covered by the focused unit tests already in this PR.

target.title: Expose model picker agent runtimes
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds additive `agentRuntime` metadata to `models.list` model choices when the model/provider has an explicit configured agent runtime.
- Shows non-default runtime labels in the WebUI model picker (for example `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`) without changing the canonical model value (`openai/gpt-5.5`).
- Adds focused gateway helper and UI label regressions for the OpenAI/Codex route split.

Refs #90420 (real 6.1 migration/runtime-observability report) and #84032 (diamond-lobster issue: model picker/provider/runtime ambiguity). Related to #90036 and #90047 because the live Windows 6.1 route incident made the same ambiguity operationally visible: the healthy route was `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex runtime, while stale durable state still surfaced as `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

## Why

After the 2026.6.1 Codex migration, a healthy route can be:

```text
model ref: openai/gpt-5.5
runtime: codex
```

The picker previously only exposed/displayed provider/model metadata, so operators could not tell whether an `openai/*` model was configured to run through Codex or through the default OpenClaw route. This PR keeps the user-facing model ref stable while exposing the configured runtime separately.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"`
- `node --import tsx` behavior script for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata` → `MODELS_LIST_RUNTIME_METADATA_OK`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`

Note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/models-list-result.test.ts --testNamePattern "runtime metadata"` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` were attempted locally but remained silent/slow in this checkout and were stopped; the focused helper behavior was verified directly and CI should run the normal suite.


## Review fix proof

Addressed ClawSweeper's implicit-runtime feedback in `a971a356`.

Focused proof from the patched branch:

```text
implicitCodex.agentRuntime = { id: "codex", source: "implicit", label: "OpenAI Codex" }
implicitDefault.agentRuntime = omitted
explicitOpenClaw.agentRuntime = { id: "openclaw", source: "model", label: "OpenClaw Default" }
labels = ["GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex", "GPT-5.5 · OpenClaw Default"]
RUNTIME_METADATA_REVIEW_FIX_OK

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime"
✓ ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped)
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped

git diff --check
```

The picker now distinguishes both default implicit OpenAI → Codex routing and explicit OpenClaw runtime overrides while still omitting compact implicit default/OpenClaw/auto metadata for unrelated providers.

## Real behavior proof (structured for gate)

- Behavior or issue addressed: expose material agent runtime metadata for `openai/gpt-*` models routed through Codex so operators can distinguish `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex from a plain OpenClaw/default runtime in the model picker.
- Real environment tested: real Windows/ROG OpenClaw `2026.6.1` migration context where the healthy route is `openai/gpt-5.5` plus Codex runtime, plus this PR branch on macOS source checkout.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran the patched WebUI picker regression with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern="agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime|surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"` and a focused helper probe for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata`.
- Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output:

```text
Focused helper output from patched branch:
implicitCodex.agentRuntime = { id: "codex", source: "implicit", label: "OpenAI Codex" }
implicitDefault.agentRuntime = omitted
explicitOpenClaw.agentRuntime = { id: "openclaw", source: "model", label: "OpenClaw Default" }
labels = ["GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex", "GPT-5.5 · OpenClaw Default"]
RUNTIME_METADATA_REVIEW_FIX_OK

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern="agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime|surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts
✓ ui ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped) 22ms
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped (25)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.78s
```

- Observed result after fix: model choices keep the canonical value `openai/gpt-5.5` while exposing/displaying `OpenAI Codex` or explicit `OpenClaw Default` runtime metadata where it materially changes operator understanding.
- What was not tested: no full browser screenshot was captured for this PR body; commentary/progress streams, provider/TaaS/cache plugin metadata, and session migration behavior are intentionally out of scope.


## Real behavior proof (Windows 6.1 route incident + patched models.list metadata)

- Behavior or issue addressed: a real upgraded Windows/Telegram OpenClaw setup used the healthy canonical model ref `openai/gpt-5.5` with Codex runtime routing, but the UI/protocol had no runtime metadata surface, making it hard to distinguish the healthy `openai/gpt-5.5 + OpenAI Codex` route from stale or default-runtime cases.
- Real environment tested: redacted Windows/ROG OpenClaw `2026.6.1 (2e08f0f)` Telegram/dashboard setup that previously hit `Unknown model: openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, plus this PR branch on a local macOS source checkout using the same canonical route shape.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: repaired the real Windows setup to canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` plus `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` without clearing OAuth, verified the real route with health/models/smoke checks, then ran this PR branch's `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata(...)` over the same redacted route shape to inspect the `models.list` choice metadata.
- Evidence after fix: copied live/redacted output from the real incident and patched branch:

```text
Real Windows 6.1 route after repair, redacted:
- previous live symptom: dashboard/Telegram turns failed with `Unknown model: openai-codex/gpt-5.5`
- repaired config/session route: modelRef = openai/gpt-5.5, agentRuntime.id = codex
- Gateway /health: live
- openclaw models list: openai/gpt-5.5 shown as default/configured
- CLI smoke: SMOKE_OK_601_MODEL_ROUTE
- Telegram direct-session smoke: TELEGRAM_SESSION_SMOKE_OK

Patched PR branch probe for the same route shape:
{
  "realRouteShape": {
    "modelRef": "openai/gpt-5.5",
    "agentRuntime": { "id": "codex" }
  },
  "patchedModelsListChoice": {
    "id": "gpt-5.5",
    "name": "GPT-5.5",
    "provider": "openai",
    "agentRuntime": {
      "id": "codex",
      "source": "model",
      "label": "OpenAI Codex"
    }
  }
}

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern="agent runtime|OpenClaw runtime|surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.ui.config.ts
✓ ui ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts (25 tests | 23 skipped) 22ms
Tests 2 passed | 23 skipped (25)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 3.78s
```

- Observed result after fix: for the real Windows route shape, the patched `models.list` choice carries `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` and label `OpenAI Codex` while preserving the canonical model choice (`provider: "openai"`, `id: "gpt-5.5"`). The picker regression confirms the UI can render that as `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` instead of hiding the runtime distinction.
- What was not tested: this PR branch was not hot-deployed to the production Windows gateway; the live Windows proof is the real incident/repair route, and the after-patch proof is the PR branch metadata/UI path exercised against that same redacted route shape. Commentary/progress streams and session-store migration/write-boundary behavior remain out of scope for this PR.

## Real behavior proof (PR branch Gateway `models.list` output)

- Behavior or issue addressed: #90420 / #84032 need the model picker and Gateway model list to show when canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` is routed through the Codex runtime, so operators can distinguish a healthy 6.1 route from stale `openai-codex/*` state.
- Real environment tested: this PR branch (`agent/xiaozhua/model-runtime-observability`, OpenClaw `2026.6.2 (ac572e7)`) running an isolated local Gateway with a temporary state dir and `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`; the config used canonical `openai/gpt-5.5` with `agents.defaults.models["openai/gpt-5.5"].agentRuntime.id = "codex"`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-gw.INQWL3 OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-gw.INQWL3/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run --port 19828 --bind loopback --auth none --allow-unconfigured --compact

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-gw.INQWL3 OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-gw.INQWL3/openclaw.json node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19828 --token dummy --json --timeout 10000

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-gw.INQWL3 OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr90328-gw.INQWL3/openclaw.json node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway call models.list --url ws://127.0.0.1:19828 --token dummy --json --timeout 30000
```

- Evidence after fix:

Gateway health loaded the PR-branch Gateway and the relevant plugins:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "plugins": {
    "loaded": ["codex", "memory-core", "openai"],
    "errors": []
  },
  "defaultAgentId": "main"
}
```

Gateway log from the same run:

```text
[gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
[gateway] http server listening (1 plugin: codex; 14.7s)
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ models.list 1137ms
```

Actual PR-branch `models.list` response:

```json
{
  "models": [
    {
      "provider": "openai",
      "id": "gpt-5.5",
      "name": "gpt-5.5",
      "agentRuntime": {
        "id": "codex",
        "source": "model",
        "label": "OpenAI Codex"
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the public Gateway model-list surface preserves the canonical selected value (`provider: "openai"`, `id: "gpt-5.5"`) while exposing the configured runtime separately as `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` / `label = "OpenAI Codex"`. This is the exact after-fix surface needed for the model picker to show `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex` without reverting to legacy `openai-codex/*` model refs.
- What was not tested: I did not use real OAuth credentials, send a model inference request, or capture a browser screenshot in this isolated proof run. Channels were intentionally skipped and the temporary Gateway was stopped after collecting the `models.list` output.

target.title: fix(discord): extend component callback ttl
target.body:
## Summary

- Raise the default Discord component callback registry TTL from 30 minutes to 7 days.
- Expand the bounded `agentComponents.ttlMs` override maximum from 24 hours to 30 days.
- Refresh Discord config docs, UI hints, generated config metadata, and baseline hashes.
- Add registry-level coverage proving the default remains usable until the 7-day boundary and expires afterward.

This keeps asynchronous review and approval cards usable by default while preserving an explicit bounded override for deployments that need a different lifetime. Intentionally out of scope: changing component authorization, reusable-component semantics, or Discord interaction handling outside registry TTLs.

Reviewers should focus on the default/max TTL choice and whether the stale-callback tradeoff is acceptable.

## Linked context

Closes #90390

Related #84184 and #84189, which added the configurable TTL field while preserving the old 30-minute default.

## Retention and safety notes

- Durable callback state remains count-bounded: persistent component and modal namespaces each open with `maxEntries: 500` plus the default TTL.
- The plugin state store sweeps expired rows and evicts oldest live rows above `maxEntries` on register, so the longer TTL does not make persistent registry storage unbounded.
- On high-volume servers, the 500-entry cap can become the effective lifetime bound before the 7-day TTL. That is an existing count-bound behavior and may be worth revisiting separately if maintainers want a different capacity tradeoff.
- The process-local in-memory registry is not the durable retention boundary, and this PR does not change its semantics.
- Component authorization and `allowedUsers` checks are unchanged. Deployments that want shorter callback lifetimes can set `agentComponents.ttlMs`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Discord component callbacks without an explicit `agentComponents.ttlMs` expired after 30 minutes by default, which is too short for asynchronous review and approval messages.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw source checkout with a standalone registry invocation, Discord registry/config/send tests, generated config metadata checks, and changed-lane type/lint checks. No live Discord multi-day click was performed.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The command imported `extensions/discord/src/components-registry.ts`, registered a component without `ttlMs`, moved the process clock to the default-TTL boundary, and resolved the entry before and after expiry.

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

Terminal output from the standalone registry invocation:

```json
{
  "source": "extensions/discord/src/components-registry.ts",
  "registeredWithoutTtlMs": true,
  "defaultTtlMs": 604800000,
  "resolvesAtSevenDaysMinusOneMs": true,
  "resolvesAtSevenDaysPlusOneMs": false,
  "observedExpiresAtDeltaMs": 604800000
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the real registry module keeps an entry registered without `ttlMs` resolvable until just before the 7-day boundary and stops resolving it just after that boundary. The focused Discord test run also passed 3 files and 67 tests.
- What was not tested: live Discord button clicks over a multi-day wall-clock interval.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the live interval is intentionally not attempted in this small PR; the standalone registry invocation and registry clock-boundary test cover the expiry logic deterministically, and send-path tests continue to cover configured TTL propagation.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current main defined `DEFAULT_COMPONENT_TTL_MS = 30 * 60 * 1000`, documented default `1800000`, and capped `agentComponents.ttlMs` at `86400000`.

## Tests and validation

Commands run after this patch:

- `pnpm test extensions/discord/src/components.test.ts extensions/discord/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/discord/src/send.components.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm lint:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `pnpm check:no-conflict-markers`

Regression coverage added or updated:

- Added a default registry TTL boundary test in `extensions/discord/src/components.test.ts`.
- Updated Discord config schema tests for the new 30-day maximum and max+1 rejection.
- Regenerated channel config metadata and config docs baseline hash.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes: stale callback lifetime increases; auth, secrets, network, and tool execution behavior are unchanged`)

Highest-risk area: longer-lived Discord component callbacks can keep old messages actionable for longer.

Mitigation: `allowedUsers` and component authorization semantics are unchanged; deployments can lower `agentComponents.ttlMs`; persistent registry state remains bounded by `maxEntries`; the schema still enforces a finite maximum, now 30 days.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review, CI, and explicit maintainer decision on the 7-day default plus 30-day maximum.

- Removed the release-owned `CHANGELOG.md` edit.
- Added source-level retention-bound documentation at the persistent Discord component/modal stores.
- Added the retention-bound explanation and latest validation evidence to this PR body.

Still waiting on: maintainer acceptance or revision of the TTL policy.

target.title: fix(gateway): skip pending reply drain on skip-deferral restart
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw gateway restart --safe --skip-deferral` skips the pre-restart active-work deferral check but still waits on the close-stage pending reply drain, because the `skipDeferral` flag is not threaded through the restart intent to the SIGUSR1 handler and server close logic.

The restart scheduler in `restart.ts` stores `skipDeferral` in module-local state (`pendingRestartSkipDeferral`) and uses it to bypass the deferral poll loop. But when it emits the SIGUSR1 restart via `emitPreparedGatewayRestart`, it passes no intent object, so `emittedRestartIntent` is `undefined`. The SIGUSR1 handler in `run-loop.ts` then gets a `restartIntent` without `skipDeferral`, and `resolveRestartCloseDrainTimeoutMs()` computes the full drain budget. The server close handler waits on pending replies from the restart-requesting turn itself.

The fix threads `skipDeferral` through the restart intent in **both** code paths:

1. **Timer path**: When `scheduledSkipDeferral` is true, passes `{ skipDeferral: true, reason }` as the intent to `emitPreparedGatewayRestart`
2. **Active-deferral bypass path**: When a `--safe --skip-deferral` request hits an already-active deferral poll, the bypass now also passes `{ skipDeferral: true, reason }` through the intent (was previously `undefined`)
3. Adds `skipDeferral?: boolean` to `GatewayRestartIntent` and `RestartIntentOptions`
4. In `resolveRestartCloseDrainTimeoutMs()`, returns `0` when `restartIntent?.skipDeferral` is true

Normal restarts (without `--skip-deferral`) are unaffected; the close drain timeout is computed from the drain budget as before.

Fixes #89528

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `--safe --skip-deferral` restart still waits on the restart-requesting turn's own pending tool/final replies because `skipDeferral` was not threaded through the restart intent to the server close logic. Additionally, a skip-deferral request arriving while a normal restart is already in the deferral poll loop would bypass the polls but still emit without the `skipDeferral` intent.

Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node v22.19, OpenClaw built from patched source at `/tmp/proof-89604`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

Step 1. Build from branch and verify fix is in compiled production code

Step 2. Verify restart scheduler threads `skipDeferral` through the intent via the timer path

Step 3. Verify restart scheduler threads `skipDeferral` through the intent via the active-deferral bypass path (a normal non-skipDeferral restart enters deferral polling, then a second skipDeferral request arrives and bypasses)

Step 4. Verify server close returns 0 drain timeout when `skipDeferral` is true

Step 5. Run tests to confirm behavior (including new active-deferral bypass test)

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the patched build:

The fix is active in the production bundle. Both code paths now thread `skipDeferral: true` through the restart intent:

```console
$ grep -n "skipDeferral" dist/restart-D_lgNyR8.js
618:if (pendingRestartPreparing && skipDeferral && activeDeferralPolls.size > 0) {
624:skipDeferral: true,
638:if (!pendingRestartPreparing && (requestedDueAt < pendingRestartDueAt || skipDeferral && !pendingRestartSkipDeferral)) {
643:pendingRestartSkipDeferral = pendingRestartSkipDeferral || skipDeferral;
661:pendingRestartSkipDeferral = skipDeferral;
673:skipDeferral: true,
```

The SIGUSR1 handler correctly returns 0 drain timeout when `skipDeferral` is set:

```console
$ grep -n -B1 -A1 "skipDeferral" dist/run-COQAnaaw.js
332:const resolveRestartCloseDrainTimeoutMs = () => {
333-if (!isRestart) return null;
385:if (restartIntent?.skipDeferral) return 0;
```

Runtime exercise of the production `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` and `consumeGatewaySigusr1RestartIntent`:

```console
$ npx tsx proof-89604.ts
=== Skip-Deferral Intent Threading Proof ===

Exercise 1: Timer path -- skipDeferral=true, no preRestartCheck
  intent: {"skipDeferral":true,"reason":"gateway.restart.safe"}
  skipDeferral present: true
  reason preserved: true

Exercise 2: Active-deferral bypass -- skipDeferral=true while deferral is polling
[restart] request bypassed active deferral reason=gateway.restart.safe pendingReason=unspecified actor=<unknown>
  intent: {"skipDeferral":true,"reason":"gateway.restart.safe"}
  skipDeferral present: true
  reason preserved: true

Exercise 3: Normal restart -- skipDeferral=false (control case)
  intent: null
  skipDeferral present: false

=== Results ===
Timer path: skipDeferral=true produces intent with skipDeferral, drain timeout -> 0
Active-deferral bypass: skipDeferral=true now threads intent (was missing before fix)
Normal restart: no skipDeferral in intent, full drain budget applies
Both code paths verified: SIGUSR1 handler receives skipDeferral for skip-deferral restarts
```

Observed result after fix: The compiled production code at `dist/restart-D_lgNyR8.js:624` and `:673` correctly threads `skipDeferral: true` through the restart intent in both the active-deferral bypass path and the timer path. The SIGUSR1 handler at `dist/run-COQAnaaw.js:385` returns drain timeout 0 when `skipDeferral` is present, bypassing the pending reply drain wait. Runtime exercise confirms the production `scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart` produces intent `{"skipDeferral":true,"reason":"gateway.restart.safe"}` through both paths. Without `skipDeferral`, the intent is `null` and the full drain budget applies.

What was not tested: Live `--safe --skip-deferral` restart with active pending replies from a channel-originated turn. The fix is verified at the intent-threading and timeout-resolution layers; end-to-end restart with live channel delivery requires configured channel credentials.
